planting.note: Broadcast seed in spring or fall at 3–5 lbs per 1000 sq ft; strawberry clover is particularly well-suited to wet, alkaline, or saline soils where white clover would struggle. Inoculate seed with appropriate rhizobium for best nitrogen fixation.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed into moist, well-prepared soil in full sun; strawberry clover closely resembles white clover (T. repens) in habit and use but tolerates three conditions where white clover fails — waterlogged soils, heavy clay, and moderate soil salinity. Named for the distinctive papery, inflated seed heads that resemble miniature strawberries after flowering, it is a valuable cover crop for poorly drained, saline, or alkaline low-lying areas such as coastal marshes, river floodplains, and irrigated fields with salinity issues. The pink flowers are attractive to honeybees and numerous native bees from spring through fall. Spreads by stolons and self-seeds freely to provide persistent cover. Fixes 50–100 lbs of atmospheric nitrogen per acre annually under good conditions. Excellent as a living mulch between vegetable rows in wetter areas.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in zone 7–9 for a winter nitrogen-fixing cover crop; barrel medic germinates in cool autumn soil and grows through mild winters, producing hard seeds that remain dormant in the soil for years and regenerate the stand without reseeding. Scarify or acid-treat seed to improve germination if using non-fresh seed.

directions: Broadcast seed in fall on prepared, well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; barrel medic is a Mediterranean annual legume that has become one of the most important self-seeding winter cover crops in warm-winter climates. The seed pods are distinctive barrel- or snail-shaped spirals with spines — an adaptation for animal dispersal. Many seeds develop a hard seed coat and remain viable in the soil for decades, creating a persistent seed bank that regenerates the cover without annual reseeding — a 'hard-seeded' trait extremely valuable in dryland farming. The plant is also the primary model organism in legume biology research (like Arabidopsis for broadleaf plants), so it is among the best-characterized plants in science. Fixes substantial nitrogen for warm-season crops that follow. In zone 9, sow September–October for winter cover; the stand dies out by late spring when soil warms.
sources: Samuel Roberts Noble Foundation Cover Crop Guide

planting.note: Scarify seeds with sandpaper or soak in hot water before sowing directly in warm soil after last frost; in zone 9, this can be treated as a fast-growing warm-season annual that self-seeds in frost-free winters. Grows best in lean, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, even sandy soil in full sun in frost-free or warm winter climates; anil indigo is a tropical American shrub historically cultivated as one of the primary sources of natural indigo dye — the leaves are harvested and fermented in water to extract the blue pigment indigotin, which was the world's most important natural blue dye before synthetic indigo was developed in the 1880s. It naturalized throughout the warm coastal Southeast after Spanish and French colonial plantations, and still grows spontaneously in disturbed areas of Florida and the Gulf Coast. In zone 9, it often behaves as a self-seeding annual or short-lived perennial; in zone 10 and warmer it is a true woody perennial. The small pink-lavender flowers in drooping racemes bloom repeatedly. Yields the same dye compound as the Asian species I. tinctoria.

planting.note: Broadcast in spring in moist, acidic, wet meadow or bog edge soil; greater birdsfoot trefoil is specifically adapted to wet, peaty, acidic conditions where common birdsfoot trefoil (L. corniculatus) does poorly. Inoculate with appropriate Rhizobium leguminosarum bv. loti for good nitrogen fixation.

directions: Broadcast seed in wet to moist, acidic, peaty or heavy clay soils in full sun; greater birdsfoot trefoil fills the ecological niche of a legume adapted to wet, acidic, low-fertility conditions — exactly the situation where most other leguminous cover crops fail due to poor drainage or low pH. Unlike common birdsfoot trefoil (L. corniculatus) which prefers well-drained soil and neutral to alkaline pH, this species grows naturally in bogs, wet meadows, pond margins, and flushes with pH as low as 5.0. The yellow flowers in summer attract bumblebees and are important in semi-natural wet meadows. Fixes nitrogen even in poorly aerated, waterlogged soils where rhizobium populations are typically suppressed. Valuable for naturalizing wet, difficult areas as a nitrogen-building groundcover.

planting.note: Scarify seeds by rubbing on sandpaper, then sow in spring in well-drained to moderately moist, lean soil; Canada milkvetch spreads by rhizomes and also self-seeds to form drifts over time in prairie and meadow conditions. Inoculate with Astragalus-specific rhizobium for best nodulation.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy to loamy, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Canada milkvetch is one of the most ecologically important native prairie legumes of eastern and central North America, fixing substantial atmospheric nitrogen through root nodule associations and providing critical pollen and nectar for bumble bees (particularly the federally threatened rusty-patched bumble bee) through the dense spikes of pale cream to yellowish flowers in summer. The plant spreads by rhizomes to form loose colonies — a beneficial trait in prairie restoration but something to manage in formal borders. The inflated seed pods are distinctive and attractive in dried arrangements. Unlike many astragalus species, Canada milkvetch is non-toxic to livestock and wildlife. An underused native alternative to introduced legume cover crops for prairie-style and meadow plantings.
